Here's an overview of common symptoms for anorexia and bulimia, keeping in mind that these are complex disorders with varying presentations:

**Anorexia Nervosa:**

* **Restriction of energy intake:** This leads to significantly low body weight relative to age, sex, developmental trajectory, and physical health.
* **Intense fear of gaining weight or becoming fat:** This fear persists even when underweight.
* **Distorted body image:** Individuals may have a distorted perception of their body size and shape, seeing themselves as overweight even when they are dangerously thin.
* **Denial of the seriousness of low body weight:** They may minimize the impact of their low weight on their health.
* **Other symptoms:**
    * Amenorrhea (loss of menstrual periods) in females
    * Fatigue, weakness, dizziness
    * Dry skin, brittle nails, hair loss
    * Cold intolerance
    * Constipation
    * Depression, anxiety, obsessive-compulsive behaviors

**Bulimia Nervosa:**

* **Recurrent episodes of binge eating:** Consuming large amounts of food in a short period of time, accompanied by a sense of loss of control.
* **Recurrent inappropriate compensatory behaviors:** These behaviors are aimed at preventing weight gain and may include:
    * Self-induced vomiting
    * Misuse of laxatives, diuretics, or enemas
    * Fasting
    * Excessive exercise
* **Self-evaluation unduly influenced by body shape and weight:**  A preoccupation with body image and weight.
* **Other symptoms:**
    * Swollen salivary glands
    * Dental problems (erosion of tooth enamel)
    * Dehydration
    * Electrolyte imbalances
    * Gastrointestinal problems (heartburn, bloating, abdominal pain)
    * Mood swings, irritability, anxiety

**Important Notes:**

* **These are just common symptoms, and not everyone with an eating disorder will experience all of them.**
* **Eating disorders can be very dangerous and even life-threatening.**
* **If you or someone you know is struggling with an eating disorder, please seek professional help.**


Remember, your blog posts can be a valuable resource for young people. By providing accurate information and promoting help-seeking behaviors, you can make a real difference.

Here's an overview of common symptoms for anorexia and bulimia, keeping in mind that these are complex disorders with varying presentations:

**Anorexia Nervosa:**

* **Restriction of energy intake:** This leads to significantly low body weight relative to age, sex, developmental trajectory, and physical health.
* **Intense fear of gaining weight or becoming fat:** This fear persists even when underweight.
* **Distorted body image:** Individuals may have a distorted perception of their body size and shape, seeing themselves as overweight even when they are dangerously thin.
* **Denial of the seriousness of low body weight:** They may minimize the impact of their low weight on their health.
* **Other symptoms:**
    * Amenorrhea (loss of menstrual periods) in females
    * Fatigue, weakness, dizziness
    * Dry skin, brittle nails, hair loss
    * Cold intolerance
    * Constipation
    * Depression, anxiety, obsessive-compulsive behaviors

**Bulimia Nervosa:**

* **Recurrent episodes of binge eating:** Consuming large amounts of food in a short period of time, accompanied by a sense of loss of control.
* **Recurrent inappropriate compensatory behaviors:** These behaviors are aimed at preventing weight gain and may include:
    * Self-induced vomiting
    * Misuse of laxatives, diuretics, or enemas
    * Fasting
    * Excessive exercise
* **Self-evaluation unduly influenced by body shape and weight:**  A preoccupation with body image and weight.
* **Other symptoms:**
    * Swollen salivary glands
    * Dental problems (erosion of tooth enamel)
    * Dehydration
    * Electrolyte imbalances
    * Gastrointestinal problems (heartburn, bloating, abdominal pain)
    * Mood swings, irritability, anxiety

**Important Notes:**

* **These are just common symptoms, and not everyone with an eating disorder will experience all of them.**
* **Eating disorders can be very dangerous and even life-threatening.**
* **If you or someone you know is struggling with an eating disorder, please seek professional help.**
